deck.title: Build The Deck The Audience Can Trust

slides.title: Build the deck the audience can trust
slides.subtitle: A polished demo of an artifact-led slide workflow.

visual_aid.purpose: Open with a strong promise without adding proof.

speaker_notes.key_message: This demo is about trust, not just generation speed.
talk_track: Frame the problem as audience trust.
Make clear the deck will separate what works today from future plans.

slides.title: The problem is not slide creation; it is trust

visual_aid.purpose: Make the trust gap visible in one glance.

validation.expected: Both panels stay balanced and sparse.
validation.fallback: Split into two slides if the contrast needs examples.

speaker_notes.key_message: Finished-looking slides can still fail on truth, clarity, and reviewability.
talk_track: Do not attack AI slide tools broadly.
Name the specific failure: weak evidence hidden behind polish.
Use this as the reason for the artifact workflow.

slides.title: The repo changes the order of work
body: Evidence and story come before rendering.
QA and export come after the browser can inspect the deck.
slides.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  A[Sources] --> B[Evidence]
  B --> C[Story]
  C --> D[Visual aids]
  D --> E[Render]
  E --> F[QA]
  F --> G[Export]

visual_aid.purpose: Show the workflow order as a simple pipeline.

validation.expected: The Mermaid diagram renders with readable labels and no crowding.
validation.fallback: Use the body copy as a static pipeline if Mermaid fails.

motion.purpose: Mark the move from problem to mechanism.
motion.reduced_motion_fallback: The slide still reads as a static flow.

talk_track: Walk left to right.
Emphasize that rendering is not the first step.
Tie the flow to cheaper repair later.

rows: Every factual claim gets an ID

visual_aid.purpose: Connect trust questions to concrete artifacts.

validation.expected: The table stays readable and uses short cells.
validation.fallback: Convert the table into three icon rows if it becomes dense.

speaker_notes.key_message: The claim ledger is the accuracy spine of the deck.
talk_track: Start with the reviewer question.
Point to the file that answers it.
Avoid implying deterministic checks replace judgment.

slides.title: Agent memory is written to files

visual_aid.purpose: Show resumable memory without describing every file.

validation.fallback: Move file examples to speaker notes.

speaker_notes.key_message: Long-running work should resume from artifacts, not chat memory.
talk_track: Explain why file-backed state matters.
Connect review-log to learning within a project.
Mention that this is ready for a future runner.

slides.title: Visual QA checks what actually rendered

visual_aid.purpose: Turn QA from an abstract promise into observable checks.

validation.expected: The table is legible and not crowded.
validation.fallback: Show checks as four short rows without table borders.

speaker_notes.key_message: Browser QA gives the agent visual feedback after rendering.
talk_track: Explain that this does not judge taste perfectly.
It catches common presentation-breaking defects.
Use screenshots for slide-by-slide review.

visual_aid.purpose: Prevent overclaiming while keeping the roadmap visible.

validation.expected: Ready and future columns remain visually distinct.
validation.fallback: Convert to a two-row roadmap if text gets cramped.

speaker_notes.key_message: The repo should be impressive because it is honest about its boundary.
talk_track: Say what works today.
Say what is deliberately future work.
Make integrity part of the product quality.

slides.title: Trust is built slide by slide
slides.quote: Make every slide earn its place, ground its claim, and help the audience move.
slides.no_claims_reason: Closing principle rather than a factual claim.

visual_aid.purpose: End with a principle the audience can remember.

speaker_notes.key_message: The repo is worth testing because it makes slide work inspectable.
talk_track: Close with the standard.
Invite review of the generated artifacts and screenshots.
Do not claim the workflow replaces human judgment.
